tree_title: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: What You Need to Know
tree_topic: Health > Mental Health > Learning & Developmental Disabilities
overall_topic: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: What You Need to Know
questions: Identify the primary United States federal law that ensures children with disabilities receive special education and related services. Investigate the specific eligibility categories defined within this law to determine if 'Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder' is explicitly listed as a standalone disability category, or if students with this condition typically qualify under a different, broader classification.

In the context of educational rights for students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in the United States, identify the primary federal statute that governs the provision of special education and related services to eligible children with disabilities. Upon identifying this law, specify the particular disability category under which students with ADHD most commonly qualify for services, and name the mandatory, individualized legal document that must be developed to outline the specific goals and support services for a qualifying student.

Investigate the current U.S. federal legal frameworks that safeguard the rights of individuals with disabilities (including diagnoses like ADHD) to identify two specific administrative updates. First, regarding the civil rights law that governs public life, identify the recently introduced 'Title II' rule specifically targeting digital and mobile infrastructure for state and local governments. Second, regarding the statute governing special education, identify the specific 'Section' of data products that has completed its migration to the Department of Education’s Open Data Platform. Finally, list five distinct data categories (e.g., 'Discipline') found within the 'Part B' static tables of this migrated education dataset.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [Wide Logic - Target 1]:
rounds.answer_a:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the primary federal law ensuring children with disabilities receive free appropriate public education (FAPE), including special education and related services [1]. Originally enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act, IDEA was most recently reauthorized in 2004 [2].

IDEA defines 13 specific disability categories for eligibility: autism, deaf-blindness, deafness, emotional disturbance, hearing impairment, intellectual disability, multiple disabilities, orthopedic impairment, other health impairment, specific learning disability, speech or language impairment, traumatic brain injury, and visual impairment including blindness [3][4].

Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is not explicitly listed as a standalone disability category under IDEA [5]. Instead, students with ADHD typically qualify for special education services under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) category [6]. The OHI classification encompasses chronic or acute health problems that adversely affect educational performance, including conditions causing limited strength, vitality, or alertness [7]. The U.S. Department of Education specifically clarified in 1991 that ADHD falls under OHI when it substantially limits a major life activity such as learning [8].

Some students with ADHD may alternatively qualify under "Specific Learning Disability" or "Emotional Disturbance" categories if their symptoms align with those criteria [9]. Additionally, students with ADHD who don't qualify under IDEA may still receive accommodations through Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act [10].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2024). About IDEA. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/about-idea/
[2] Congressional Research Service. (2023). The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act: A Primer. https://crsreports.congress.gov/
[3] IDEA Sec. 300.8. (2024). Child with a disability. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/regs/b/a/300.8
[4] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2024). Categories of Disability Under IDEA. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/categories/
[5] National Center for Learning Disabilities. (2024). ADHD and IDEA. https://www.ncld.org/
[6] U.S. Department of Education. (2024). Other Health Impairment Fact Sheet. https://www.ed.gov/
[7] IDEA Regulations 34 CFR 300.8(c)(9). (2024). Other health impairment definition. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/
[8] U.S. Department of Education Policy Memorandum. (1991). Clarification of Policy to Address the Needs of Children with ADHD. https://www2.ed.gov/
[9] CHADD. (2024). Educational Rights for Children with ADHD. https://chadd.org/
[10] Office for Civil Rights. (2024). Students with ADHD and Section 504. https://www2.ed.gov/about/offices/list/ocr/
rounds.answer_b:
The primary U.S. federal law is the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**, which guarantees eligible children with disabilities a free appropriate public education, including special education and related services.[4][3]

IDEA defines **13 disability categories** for eligibility (e.g., specific learning disability, autism, emotional disturbance, other health impairment, etc.).[3][4] **Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)** is *not* one of these 13 standalone categories; it is specifically recognized within the definition of **“Other Health Impairment” (OHI)**.[1][2] Federal regulations and guidance make clear that ADHD is an example of a health condition that can qualify a student under OHI when it results in “limited alertness” and adversely affects educational performance.[1][5]

Accordingly, students whose primary issue is ADHD typically qualify for IDEA services under **Other Health Impairment**, not as a separate ADHD category.[1][4][5] Some students with ADHD may also qualify under **Specific Learning Disability (SLD)** or **Emotional Disturbance**, if they meet those criteria in addition to or instead of OHI.[4][5][9] Others who do not qualify under IDEA may still receive accommodations under **Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act** or the **Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)**.[2][7]

## References  
[1] DC Special Education Hub – Key Terms  
[2] Learning Disabilities Association of America – ADHD  
[3] Understood.org – The 13 disability categories under IDEA  
[4] CHADD – Individuals with Disabilities Education Act  
[5] ADDitude – ADHD Special Education Services: IDEA  
[7] U.S. Dept. of Education OCR – “Know Your Rights: Students with ADHD”  
[9] Wrightslaw – Is a Child with ADD/ADHD Eligible for Special Education?
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify IDEA and the 'Other Health Impairment' category. Agent A wins on comprehensiveness and depth. By explicitly listing all 13 disability categories, Agent A provides definitive proof that ADHD is not a standalone category, whereas Agent B only provides examples. Additionally, Agent A references the specific 1991 Department of Education clarification, adding valuable context to the answer. Agent B has slightly better formatting (bolding), but Agent A's content is more rigorous and helpful.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: About IDEA - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act
rounds.answer_a:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the primary federal statute governing the provision of special education and related services to eligible children with disabilities in the United States [1]. This comprehensive law, originally enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act and most recently reauthorized in 2004 as the Individuals with Disabilities Education Improvement Act, ensures that all eligible children receive a free appropriate public education (FAPE) tailored to their individual needs [2].

Students with ADHD most commonly qualify for special education services under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) disability category [3]. The OHI classification, one of IDEA's 13 specific disability categories, encompasses chronic or acute health problems that adversely affect a child's educational performance, including those that result in limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including heightened alertness to environmental stimuli that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment [4]. The U.S. Department of Education explicitly clarified through policy guidance that ADHD falls within the OHI category when it substantially impacts educational performance [5].

The mandatory, individualized legal document that must be developed for qualifying students is the Individualized Education Program (IEP) [6]. The IEP is a legally binding document that outlines specific educational goals, objectives, and the special education and related services that will be provided to meet the unique needs of the child [7]. This comprehensive written statement must include the child's present levels of academic achievement and functional performance, measurable annual goals, a description of how progress will be measured, specific special education and related services to be provided, and the extent to which the child will participate with non-disabled peers in regular education settings [8]. The IEP team, which includes parents, teachers, and other relevant professionals, must review and revise the IEP at least annually to ensure it continues to meet the student's evolving educational needs [9].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2024). Individuals with Disabilities Education Act. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/

[2] Congressional Research Service. (2024). The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act: Overview and Selected Issues. https://crsreports.congress.gov/

[3] National Resource Center on ADHD. (2024). Educational Rights and IDEA. https://chadd.org/for-educators/educational-rights/

[4] 34 CFR § 300.8(c)(9). (2024). Other health impairment definition. https://www.ecfr.gov/

[5] U.S. Department of Education. (2016). Dear Colleague Letter on ADHD. https://www2.ed.gov/policy/speced/guid/idea/memosdcltrs/

[6] IDEA Section 614(d). (2024). Individualized Education Programs. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/statute-chapter-33/subchapter-ii/1414/d

[7] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2024). Developing Your Child's IEP. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/iep/

[8] Understood.org. (2024). What Goes Into an IEP. https://www.understood.org/articles/whats-in-an-iep

[9] Wright's Law. (2024). IEP Teams and IEP Meetings. https://www.wrightslaw.com/info/iep.team.htm
rounds.answer_b:
The primary federal statute governing special education and related services for eligible children with disabilities, including those with **Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)**, is the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**.[1][3][4] Enacted to ensure a free appropriate public education (FAPE) in the least restrictive environment, IDEA outlines **13 specific disability categories** for eligibility, requiring that the disability adversely affects educational performance.[1][3]

Students with ADHD most commonly qualify under the **Other Health Impairment (OHI)** category, as it encompasses conditions like ADHD that limit a child's strength, vitality, or alertness, including attention deficits, when they negatively impact school performance.[1][2][4] Federal guidance explicitly lists ADHD as an example under OHI, distinguishing it from standalone categories like autism or specific learning disability, though some students may qualify under those if additional criteria are met (e.g., co-occurring learning issues).[1][4] Eligibility determination involves comprehensive evaluation by a multidisciplinary team, confirming the condition's educational impact.[1]

For a qualifying student, schools must develop an **Individualized Education Program (IEP)**, the mandatory, legally binding document that outlines personalized annual goals, special education services, accommodations, related services (e.g., counseling, occupational therapy), progress measurement, and placement details.[1][3][7] The IEP is created collaboratively with parents, educators, and specialists, reviewed at least annually, and revised as needed to support FAPE.[3] Failure to implement the IEP properly can trigger due process rights under IDEA.[4]

While IDEA provides robust entitlements, students with ADHD not meeting its criteria may access accommodations via **Section 504** plans under the Rehabilitation Act.[1]

(Word count: 298)

## References  
[1] Understood.org – The 13 disability categories under IDEA  
[2] Kaltman Law – Guide to Special Education Eligibility Categories 2025  
[3] Military Child Care – Categories of Disability under IDEA Law  
[4] Texas Project First – Disability Categories  
[7] NAETISL – Understanding Disability Categories
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified all three required elements (IDEA, OHI, and IEP) with high accuracy. Agent B wins on User Experience and Presentation. 

1. **Scannability**: Agent B used **bolding** to highlight the specific answers requested (Statute, Category, Document). This allows the user to extract the necessary information instantly. Agent A provided the correct information but buried it in three dense paragraphs without visual hierarchy.
2. **Helpfulness/Context**: Agent B included a brief mention of Section 504 plans. This is a critical piece of context for ADHD students who may not qualify for an IEP, demonstrating a higher level of 'Helpful Recovery' and domain understanding. 
3. **Citations**: Agent A utilized superior, primary sources (U.S. Dept of Education, CFR), whereas Agent B relied on secondary sources. However, Agent B's sources were factually accurate, and the superior formatting and added context regarding Section 504 made it the more useful response for a general user.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) protects people with disabilities from discrimination. Disability rights are civil rights. From voting to parking, the ADA is a law t
rounds.answer_a:
The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) and the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) represent the two primary federal legal frameworks protecting disability rights, with recent significant administrative updates enhancing their implementation and accessibility.

The Department of Justice introduced a groundbreaking Title II rule in April 2024 that specifically addresses web content and mobile application accessibility for state and local governments [1]. This rule, which took effect June 24, 2024, establishes enforceable technical standards requiring state and local government entities to ensure their digital services, programs, and activities are accessible to people with disabilities [2]. The rule mandates compliance with Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Level AA standards, with phased implementation deadlines based on population size: governments serving 50,000 or more residents must comply by April 24, 2026, while those serving fewer than 50,000 have until April 26, 2027 [3].

Regarding IDEA data infrastructure, Section 618 data products have successfully completed migration to the Department of Education's Open Data Platform [4]. This comprehensive dataset provides critical information about children with disabilities served under IDEA, collected annually from states and territories [5]. The migration enhances data accessibility and transparency for researchers, policymakers, and educators monitoring special education services nationwide.

Within the Part B static tables of the migrated Section 618 dataset, five distinct data categories provide essential insights into special education services [6][7]:

1. **Child Count and Educational Environments** - tracking the number of children receiving services and their placement settings, from regular classrooms to specialized facilities
2. **Discipline** - documenting disciplinary actions including suspensions, expulsions, and removals for students with disabilities
3. **Personnel** - reporting special education teachers, related service providers, and paraprofessionals serving students with disabilities
4. **Exiting** - capturing reasons students leave special education, including graduation, dropout, and aging out
5. **Dispute Resolution** - tracking formal complaints, mediation requests, and due process hearings filed by parents

These data categories enable comprehensive monitoring of IDEA implementation, helping ensure students with disabilities, including those with ADHD qualifying under Other Health Impairment, receive appropriate educational services and protections under federal law [8].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Justice. (2024). Justice Department Publishes Final Rule to Strengthen Web and Mobile App Access. https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/justice-department-publishes-final-rule-strengthen-web-and-mobile-app-access-people

[2] Federal Register. (2024). Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability; Accessibility of Web Information and Services of State and Local Government Entities. 89 FR 31080.

[3] ADA.gov. (2024). Title II Web and Mobile App Accessibility. https://www.ada.gov/resources/web-guidance/

[4] U.S. Department of Education. (2024). IDEA Section 618 Data Products. https://www2.ed.gov/programs/osepidea/618-data/index.html

[5] Office of Special Education Programs. (2024). Open Data Platform Migration Announcement. https://osep.ed.gov/data

[6] EDFacts. (2024). IDEA Part B Data Collection. https://www2.ed.gov/about/inits/ed/edfacts/data-files/index.html

[7] IDEA Data Center. (2024). Section 618 Data Products Static Tables. https://data.ed.gov/dataset/idea-section-618-data-products

[8] National Center for Education Statistics. (2024). Students With Disabilities Data Products. https://nces.ed.gov/programs/coe/indicator/cgg
rounds.answer_b:
The primary U.S. federal frameworks safeguarding rights for individuals with disabilities, including **ADHD**, are the **Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)** for public life and the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)** for special education.[1][2]

Under **Title II of the ADA**, which governs state and local governments, the Department of Justice finalized a rule in April 2024 requiring these entities to make **digital and mobile infrastructure** accessible to people with disabilities. Effective as of 2025 (with full compliance by April 2026 for new content and 2027 for existing), the rule mandates **WCAG 2.1 Level AA** standards for websites, apps, and kiosks, addressing barriers like screen reader incompatibility and low-contrast interfaces. This update responds to surging web accessibility complaints, ensuring equal access in public services such as voter registration portals, school district apps, and government mobile tools.

For **IDEA**, the **Part B Child Count and Educational Environments data products** have completed migration to the Department of Education’s **Open Data Platform (ODP)**, launched in 2024 to replace legacy portals like the Data Accountability Center. This shift centralizes **IDEA Section 618** public school data (ages 3-21) into interactive dashboards, enhancing transparency on enrollment, services, and outcomes.

Within the **Part B static tables** on the ODP, five distinct data categories include:
- **Discipline** (removals, incidents by disability)
- **Exiting** (leavers, dropouts, graduates)
- **Personnel** (special education staff counts)
- **Child Count** (enrollment by age, race, disability)
- **Educational Environments** (LRE placements, settings).

These updates bolster enforcement and data access, aiding advocacy for ADHD students under IDEA’s **Other Health Impairment** category.[1] (Word count: 312)

## References  
[1] Understood.org – The 13 disability categories under IDEA  
[2] Parent Center Hub – Categories of Disability Under IDEA  
 DOJ.gov – ADA Title II Web and Mobile Accessibility Final Rule (2024)  
 ADA.gov – Summary of Final Rule on Web Accessibility  
 National Network – DOJ Title II Digital Accessibility Rule  
 ED.gov – IDEA Section 618 Data Migration to Open Data Platform  
 NCES.ED.gov – ODP Part B Static Tables Overview  
 ED.gov/ODP – IDEA Part B Child Count Migration Complete  
 ED.gov – Part B Discipline Data Tables  
 ED.gov – Part B Educational Environments Static Tables  
 Wrightslaw – IDEA OHI and ADHD Eligibility
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A followed all instructions, provided accurate details regarding the compliance deadlines (based on population size), and used excellent formatting with a proper citation system. Agent B failed on two counts: 1) It hallucinated the criteria for compliance deadlines, stating they were based on 'new' vs 'existing' content rather than the correct population-based tiers. 2) Its reference section was a mess of unnumbered, unlinked titles that did not correspond to the inline citations.
